title: The Insult Owed

giver.name: Guthry Pell, once a Hearth-Warden's apprentice
giver.wound: Prayed to Halor every night for a season while his brother lay dying of a wound the Withdrawal's chaos left untended too long — clean prayers, proper offerings, everything the god is owed. His brother died anyway. Guthry has never told a priest he thinks Halor simply didn't bother to answer.

steps.text: Guthry asks you, flatly, to lay a piece of crab-meat on Halor's altar — something the god openly can't stand, by every account of his appetites. Not as a joke. He wants Halor insulted, deliberately, on the record, and he'd rather it be done by someone who wasn't there for his brother's death and won't flinch doing it.

You go with him to the smokehouse where his brother died, still standing, still in use, the exact hearth Guthry prayed beside for a season straight. He hasn't set foot inside it since. He wants you to see it before you decide whether to do what he's asking.

Guthry doesn't actually want Halor insulted — he wants someone, anyone, to finally treat his grief as a real grievance against a real god instead of an unfortunate accident nobody's at fault for. The offense is a stand-in for a conversation he's never had the nerve to start with a priest directly: was his brother's death Halor's failure, or just the world's, and does the god even owe him an answer either way.

options.text: Lay the crab-meat on the altar as asked. Let the offense stand as the honest, angry prayer it actually is.

ledger.note: a deliberate offense, taken with real grief behind it

options.text: Talk him into speaking with a priest instead — bring his actual question to someone who can at least sit with it, rather than settling for a gesture at an altar.

ledger.note: trading a symbolic wound for a real conversation

steps.text: You lay the crab-meat on the Altar of the Salt-Father in front of Guthry. Nothing dramatic happens — no thunder, no visible wrath — which somehow makes it worse and better at once. Guthry stands there a long moment, then finally, quietly, says his brother's name out loud for the first time in years.

The Hearth-Warden hears Guthry out without flinching, and gives him an answer that isn't comfort exactly: 'Halor keeps what's kept well. Your brother wasn't kept well — not by the god, not by the world that broke that season, not by any of us. That's not a verdict on you or him. It's just true.' Guthry doesn't look forgiven. He looks, for the first time, believed.

echo.text: Offend: the altar's offense is recorded plainly in the chapel's ledger, and Guthry starts visiting the smokehouse again, slowly, on his own terms. Priest: Guthry becomes a regular presence at chapel, not as a devotee exactly, but as someone finally allowed to be angry at a god in front of the god's own people.

curatorNote: The 'someone wants a god insulted' ask, using Halor's actual offends-lane item (item-crab-meat, per gods/halor.json). Turn: the offense is really a stand-in for an ungrieved anger the giver has never spoken aloud. Deliberately no god-favor reward — a calling built around insulting Halor shouldn't hand out his favor regardless of branch chosen.
